schools.summary: Israel journeying to Rephidim, where there was no water: 'and all the congregation of the children of Israel journeyed from the wilderness of Sin, after their journeys, according to the commandment of the LORD, and pitched in Rephidim: and there was no water for the people to drink' — Israel journeying by stages (as the LORD directed, by the cloud, Numbers 9:18) and camping at Rephidim (the march under God's direction, the providence guiding the people); the journeying 'at the commandment of the LORD' (by the pillar, Numbers 9:18) — the people led by God (the providence ordering the march, even to a place of trial); 'there was no water for the people to drink' (the want of water, the trial at Rephidim, the providence, God leading them, by his own command, to a place of need, to try their faith, and to give the water from the rock, vv.6, the test of the wilderness) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And all the congregation of the children of Israel journeyed from the wilderness of Sin... and pitched in Rephidim: and there was no water for the people to drink
Israel journeying to Rephidim, where there was no water
the march under God's direction (Numbers 9:18)
led by God's command to a place of trial, the test of faith (vv.6)

schools.summary: The people chiding with Moses and tempting the LORD: 'wherefore the people did chide with Moses, and said, Give us water that we may drink. And Moses said unto them, Why chide ye with me? wherefore do ye tempt the LORD?' — the people, lacking water (v.1), quarreling (chiding, contending) with Moses, demanding water (the murmuring, the recurring unbelief, as at the Red Sea, Exodus 14:11; the bitter waters, 15:24; the manna, 16:2, the contention with the leader); Moses' answer ('why chide ye with me?') — the people's quarrel really against God (Moses but his servant, the rebuke of the murmuring, the misdirected complaint); 'wherefore do ye tempt the LORD?' (the people's chiding a 'tempting' of the LORD, putting God to the test, doubting his presence and care, demanding proof, v.7; Psalm 78:18-20, 'they tempted God in their heart'; 95:9, 'when your fathers tempted me,' the sin of unbelief, questioning whether the LORD was among them, v.7, the provoking of God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: Wherefore the people did chide with Moses... Why chide ye with me? wherefore do ye tempt the LORD?
the people chiding with Moses and tempting the LORD
the murmuring really against God, the misdirected complaint
the tempting of God's presence and care, the sin of unbelief (Psalm 78:18; 95:9)

schools.summary: The people's thirst and bitter murmuring: 'and the people thirsted there for water; and the people murmured against Moses, and said, Wherefore is this that thou hast brought us up out of Egypt, to kill us and our children and our cattle with thirst?' — the people, pressed by thirst, murmuring bitterly against Moses (the recurring murmuring, the unbelief of the wilderness, the contention bred of the want); the bitter reproach (blaming Moses for bringing them out to die of thirst) — the ingratitude (as at the Red Sea, Exodus 14:11, the murmuring of unbelief, forgetting the late wonders, the manna, the water of Marah); 'to kill us and our children and our cattle with thirst' (the exaggerated complaint, charging Moses, and God, with murderous intent, the failing of faith, the people not trusting God to provide, despite the manna, the provoking murmur, that God would yet answer with the water from the rock, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And the people thirsted there for water; and the people murmured against Moses... to kill us and our children and our cattle with thirst?
the people's thirst and bitter murmuring
the ingratitude and unbelief, blaming Moses (as Exodus 14:11)
soon answered by the water from the rock (vv.6)

schools.summary: Moses crying to the LORD in his distress: 'and Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, What shall I do unto this people? they be almost ready to stone me' — Moses, in the crisis (the people's fury, near to stoning him), crying to the LORD for help (the right resort, the leader carrying the trouble to God, the intercessor's prayer, the danger, the people ready to stone him, the violence of the murmuring); Moses' perplexity (not knowing what to do, the people ungovernable) — the dependence on God (the cry of the troubled servant); 'they be almost ready to stone me' (the people's fury, near to violence against Moses, the peril of the leader, the providence, the cry that God would answer with the water from the rock, vv.5-6, the danger driving Moses to God) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, What shall I do unto this people? they be almost ready to stone me
Moses crying to the LORD in his distress
the right resort - the leader carrying the trouble to God
the people's fury near to violence, soon answered (vv.5-6)

schools.summary: God's command to Moses to take the rod and the elders: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Go on before the people, and take with thee of the elders of Israel; and thy rod, wherewith thou smotest the river, take in thine hand, and go' — God answering Moses' cry (v.4): bidding him go before the people with the elders (as witnesses) and the rod (the instrument of the wonders, with which he smote the Nile, Exodus 7:20, the preparation for the miracle, the rod and the elders, the sign and the witnesses); the elders taken (as witnesses of the miracle) — the credible testimony (the elders to see the wonder); 'thy rod, wherewith thou smotest the river, take in thine hand, and go' (the rod, the instrument of the plagues, Exodus 7:20, now to bring water from the rock, the sign of God's power, the providence, the rod that brought judgment now bringing mercy, the water from the rock, v.6) — Wesley, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Go on before the people, and take with thee of the elders of Israel; and thy rod, wherewith thou smotest the river, take in thine hand, and go
God's command to Moses to take the rod and the elders
the elders as witnesses, the rod as the sign
the rod that brought judgment (Exodus 7:20) now to bring mercy (v.6)

schools.summary: The rock smitten and the water given: 'behold, I will stand before thee there upon the rock in Horeb; and thou shalt smite the rock, and there shall come water out of it, that the people may drink. And Moses did so in the sight of the elders of Israel' — God promising to stand (be present) on the rock at Horeb, and bidding Moses smite it with the rod, that water might come out for the people (the divine presence at the rock, the miracle, water from the flinty rock, the gift of life in the desert); the rock smitten, water flowing — the witnesses with one voice noting the great fulfillment in Christ: 'they drank of that spiritual Rock that followed them: and that Rock was Christ' (1 Corinthians 10:4, the smitten rock a type of Christ smitten, the rock struck with the rod, as Christ smitten by the law and the justice of God, Isaiah 53:4-5, 'smitten of God, and afflicted,' the water flowing from the smitten rock the type of the living water, the Spirit and grace, flowing from the crucified Christ, John 7:37-39, 'out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water'; John 19:34, the blood and water from his pierced side, the rock smitten once, the later rock to be spoken to, not smitten, Numbers 20:8-11, Christ once offered, Hebrews 9:28, the gift of life from the smitten Rock, 'God standing on the rock,' the LORD himself in the rock, the deity in Christ, the spiritual drink of the redeemed); 'that the people may drink. And Moses did so in the sight of the elders' (the water given for the murmuring people, the grace abounding to the unworthy, the rock smitten for the rebellious, the supply of life, the abundant water, Psalm 78:15-16, 20; 105:41, the providence, the gift confirmed before the elders, the type of Christ the living water) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

debates.question: What does the smitten rock signify?

sides.position: A type of Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4, 'that Rock was Christ') - the rock smitten as Christ smitten (Isaiah 53:4-5), the living water flowing from the crucified Christ (John 7:37-39; 19:34), the rock smitten once (Hebrews 9:28); the gift of life from the smitten Rock, the spiritual drink of the redeemed

themes: Behold, I will stand before thee there upon the rock in Horeb; and thou shalt smite the rock, and there shall come water out of it, that the people may drink
the rock smitten and the water given (the crux of the smitten Rock, a type of Christ)
the smitten rock a type of Christ smitten (1 Corinthians 10:4; Isaiah 53:4-5)
the living water flowing from the crucified Christ (John 7:37-39; 19:34), the rock smitten once (Hebrews 9:28)

schools.summary: The place named Massah and Meribah for the people's tempting: 'and he called the name of the place Massah, and Meribah, because of the chiding of the children of Israel, and because they tempted the LORD, saying, Is the LORD among us, or not?' — Moses naming the place Massah (temptation) and Meribah (strife), commemorating the people's chiding and tempting of the LORD (the memorial of the sin, the place a lasting witness to the murmuring, the names of the rebellion); the tempting of the LORD (the sin, Psalm 95:8-9, 'harden not your heart, as in the provocation, and as in the day of temptation in the wilderness'; quoted, Hebrews 3:8-9) — the provoking of God by unbelief (the sin remembered as a warning, the place a perpetual caution against tempting God); 'Is the LORD among us, or not?' (the heart of the tempting, doubting God's presence and care, despite all the wonders, the unbelief, questioning whether God was with them, the sin, the people demanding proof, after all the signs, the warning, Psalm 95; Hebrews 3:7-11, the wilderness generation a warning to all) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he called the name of the place Massah, and Meribah... because they tempted the LORD, saying, Is the LORD among us, or not?
the place named Massah and Meribah for the people's tempting
the memorial of the murmuring and unbelief (Psalm 95:8-9; Hebrews 3:8-9)
'Is the LORD among us?' - the unbelief, a lasting warning against tempting God (Hebrews 3:7-11)

schools.summary: Amalek attacking Israel at Rephidim: 'then came Amalek, and fought with Israel in Rephidim' — Amalek (the descendants of Esau, Genesis 36:12, a fierce, predatory desert people) coming and fighting against Israel at Rephidim (the first armed enemy after the Exodus, the unprovoked attack, falling on the rear, the feeble and weary, Deuteronomy 25:17-18, the foe of God's people); Amalek's attack (the cruel, cowardly assault on the weary people) — the first of Israel's enemies (the type, the fathers note, of the enemies of God's people, the flesh and the powers of darkness warring against the redeemed, Galatians 5:17, the perpetual foe); 'in Rephidim' (at the place of the water-trial, vv.1-7, the trial of war following the trial of thirst, the providence, the enemy permitted to test and exercise Israel, and to be the occasion of the victory by Moses' uplifted hands, vv.9-13, and the perpetual war, vv.14-16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Then came Amalek, and fought with Israel in Rephidim
Amalek attacking Israel at Rephidim
the first armed enemy after the Exodus, the cruel attack on the weary (Deuteronomy 25:17-18)
a type of the enemies of God's people (Galatians 5:17)

schools.summary: Moses sending Joshua to fight while he holds the rod: 'and Moses said unto Joshua, Choose us out men, and go out, fight with Amalek: to morrow I will stand on the top of the hill with the rod of God in mine hand' — Moses appointing Joshua (here first named, the future leader, Numbers 27:18) to choose men and fight Amalek (the use of means, the battle to be fought, the men armed, Joshua the captain, the warrior, a type, the fathers note, of Jesus, the same name, the captain of salvation, leading God's people against their foes, Hebrews 2:10); the use of means (the battle fought by Joshua and the men) joined to the prayer of Moses (v.11) — the lawful means and the prayer together (the war of God's people); 'I will stand on the top of the hill with the rod of God in mine hand' (Moses on the hill with the rod, the rod of God, the instrument of the wonders, Exodus 4:20; 7:20; 17:5 — holding it up in prayer and as the sign of God's power, the victory depending not on the sword but on the uplifted rod and hands, vv.11, the intercession, Moses praying on the hill while Joshua fought, the providence, the means and the prayer, the victory of God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Moses said unto Joshua, Choose us out men, and go out, fight with Amalek: to morrow I will stand on the top of the hill with the rod of God in mine hand
Moses sending Joshua to fight while he holds the rod
the lawful means (Joshua's battle) joined to the prayer of Moses (v.11)
Joshua the captain, a type of Jesus the captain of salvation (Hebrews 2:10)

schools.summary: Joshua fighting while Moses, Aaron, and Hur go up the hill: 'so Joshua did as Moses had said to him, and fought with Amalek: and Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill' — Joshua obeying, fighting Amalek (the use of means), while Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up the hill (Moses to hold up the rod in prayer, v.11, Aaron and Hur to support him, v.12, the obedience of Joshua, the going-up to pray, the battle below, the prayer above); Joshua's obedience (fighting the battle) — the lawful means (the warrior at his task); 'Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill' (Moses going up to pray, with the rod, v.11, Aaron and Hur with him, to support his hands, v.12, the intercession, the battle won by prayer above as much as by the sword below, the providence, the prayer and the means together) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: So Joshua did as Moses had said to him, and fought with Amalek: and Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill
Joshua fighting while Moses, Aaron, and Hur go up the hill
the battle below, the intercession above (v.11)
the means and the prayer joined

schools.summary: The victory tied to Moses' uplifted hands: 'and it came to pass, when Moses held up his hand, that Israel prevailed: and when he let down his hand, Amalek prevailed' — the great sign — 'when Moses held up his hand, that Israel prevailed: and when he let down his hand, Amalek prevailed': the battle's tide rising and falling with Moses' hands (when he held them up, Israel prevailing, when he let them down, Amalek prevailing, the witnesses noting the lifted hands as a gesture of prayer, the uplifting of hands the posture of prayer and intercession, Psalm 28:2; 63:4; 134:2; 1 Timothy 2:8, 'lifting up holy hands,' Moses interceding with uplifted hands, and the rod, v.9 — the victory depending on prayer, not on the sword alone, the lesson — the battles of God's people won by prayer, the intercession of the man of God prevailing, the power of prayer in the conflict, as the church's victory hangs on prayer, when prayer flags, the enemy prevails, the need of persevering, unwearied prayer, the type, the fathers note, of Christ's continual intercession, Hebrews 7:25, ever living to make intercession, and of the believer's dependence on prayer in the spiritual warfare, Ephesians 6:18); when Moses' hands sank (weary, v.12), Amalek prevailing — the lesson (when prayer flags, the enemy gains, the need of constancy in prayer, the dependence of the victory on the unceasing intercession); the victory tied to the uplifted hands — the power of prayer (the battle God's, won by intercession, the providence, God teaching that the deliverance is by prayer and his power, not by might, Zechariah 4:6, the encouragement to prayer) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill, Clarke.

debates.question: What do Moses' uplifted hands signify?

sides.position: A gesture of prayer and intercession (Psalm 28:2; 1 Timothy 2:8) - the victory depending on prayer, not the sword; the battles of God's people won by prayer, lost when prayer flags; the need of persevering, supported prayer (v.12), a type of Christ's continual intercession (Hebrews 7:25) and the believer's dependence on prayer (Ephesians 6:18)

themes: And it came to pass, when Moses held up his hand, that Israel prevailed: and when he let down his hand, Amalek prevailed
the victory tied to Moses' uplifted hands (the crux of prevailing prayer)
the uplifted hands a gesture of prayer (Psalm 28:2; 1 Timothy 2:8), the battle won by intercession
a type of Christ's continual intercession (Hebrews 7:25) and the believer's dependence on prayer (Ephesians 6:18)

schools.summary: Aaron and Hur staying up Moses' weary hands: 'but Moses' hands were heavy; and they took a stone, and put it under him, and he sat thereon; and Aaron and Hur stayed up his hands, the one on the one side, and the other on the other side; and his hands were steady until the going down of the sun' — Moses' hands growing weary (heavy) with the long holding-up, so Aaron and Hur setting a stone for him to sit, and holding up his hands on either side (the support of the intercessor, Moses' weariness, the helpers staying his hands, the lesson, the man of prayer needing support, the weakness of the flesh in the long intercession); Aaron and Hur supporting Moses' hands — the help of brethren in prayer (the supporting of the intercessor, the lesson, the value of mutual help in prayer, the holding-up of one another's hands, Galatians 6:2, bearing one another's burdens, the strengthening of the hands that hang down, Hebrews 12:12); 'his hands were steady until the going down of the sun' (the hands held up, steady, firm, till sunset, the prayer sustained to the end, the perseverance in prayer, the victory secured by the unwearied intercession, supported by the helpers, the providence, the prayer maintained, the battle won, v.13) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: But Moses' hands were heavy... and Aaron and Hur stayed up his hands, the one on the one side, and the other on the other side; and his hands were steady until the going down of the sun
Aaron and Hur staying up Moses' weary hands
the support of the intercessor, the help of brethren in prayer (Galatians 6:2; Hebrews 12:12)
the perseverance in prayer that secured the victory (v.13)

schools.summary: Joshua's victory over Amalek: 'and Joshua discomfited Amalek and his people with the edge of the sword' — Joshua defeating (discomfiting, overthrowing) Amalek and his army with the sword (the victory won, by the means, the sword, and the prayer, vv.11-12, the overthrow of the enemy, the deliverance of God's people); the victory by the sword (the lawful means) joined to the prayer (Moses' hands, vv.11-12) — the means and the prayer together (the enemy overthrown); 'with the edge of the sword' (the victory by the sword, yet won by prayer, vv.11, the deliverance from the first foe, the providence, the battle the LORD's, won by prayer and means, the type of the spiritual warfare, Ephesians 6:17-18, the sword of the Spirit and prayer) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley, Clarke.

themes: And Joshua discomfited Amalek and his people with the edge of the sword
Joshua's victory over Amalek
the enemy overthrown by the sword and the uplifted hands (vv.11-12)
the battle the LORD's, a type of the spiritual warfare (Ephesians 6:17-18)

schools.summary: The doom of Amalek written for a memorial: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Write this for a memorial in a book, and rehearse it in the ears of Joshua: for I will utterly put out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven' — God commanding Moses to write the matter (the victory and the doom of Amalek) in a book (the first mention of writing Scripture, the inspired record) and rehearse it to Joshua (who would have to do with Amalek, the memorial, the lasting record, the commission, the doom of Amalek committed to Joshua, and the future, Deuteronomy 25:17-19; 1 Samuel 15); the writing in a book (the inspired record, the first express command to write Scripture) — the perpetuating of God's word and works (the memorial for the generations); 'I will utterly put out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven' (the doom of Amalek, the total destruction decreed, for their unprovoked cruelty against God's people, Deuteronomy 25:17-19, fulfilled in part by Saul, 1 Samuel 15, and David, 1 Samuel 30, the remnant destroyed, 1 Chronicles 4:43, the judgment on the enemy of God's people, the type of the final destruction of all God's enemies, the perpetual war, v.16) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Write this for a memorial in a book... for I will utterly put out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven
the doom of Amalek written for a memorial
the first command to write Scripture, the inspired record
the doom of Amalek decreed for their cruelty (Deuteronomy 25:17-19; 1 Samuel 15)

schools.summary: Moses building the altar Jehovah-Nissi: 'and Moses built an altar, and called the name of it Jehovahnissi' — Moses building an altar (in thanksgiving and memorial of the victory) and naming it (the altar of praise, giving God the glory of the victory, the memorial of the deliverance); the name — 'Jehovahnissi' ('the LORD is my banner'): Moses naming the altar 'the LORD my banner' (Hebrew nes, a banner, standard, ensign, the LORD himself Israel's banner, the standard under which they fought and conquered, the rallying-point and the cause of the victory, the witnesses noting the lesson — the LORD the banner of his people, the standard of their warfare, the ensign for the nations, Isaiah 11:10, 12, the banner of love, Song of Songs 2:4, Christ lifted up as the banner, John 12:32, the cross the banner of the redeemed, the victory ascribed to the LORD, not to the sword, but to God their banner, the praise of the deliverer, the altar a confession that the LORD won the battle); 'Jehovahnissi' (the praise of God for the victory, the LORD the banner, the giver of the triumph, the providence, the altar a memorial that the LORD fights for his people, the banner of their war, the confession of faith) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does the name 'Jehovah-Nissi' (the LORD my banner) confess?

sides.position: The LORD himself Israel's banner - the standard of their warfare, the cause of the victory, the victory ascribed to God not the sword; the LORD the ensign for the nations (Isaiah 11:10), Christ lifted up as the banner (John 12:32), the cross the banner of the redeemed

themes: And Moses built an altar, and called the name of it Jehovahnissi
Moses building the altar Jehovah-Nissi (the crux of 'the LORD my banner')
the LORD himself Israel's banner, the victory ascribed to God not the sword
the ensign for the nations (Isaiah 11:10), Christ lifted up as the banner (John 12:32)

schools.summary: The LORD's perpetual war with Amalek: 'for he said, Because the LORD hath sworn that the LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation' — the LORD swearing (or, the hand upon the throne of the LORD, lifting his hand to his throne in oath) perpetual war against Amalek from generation to generation (the divine enmity, God himself at war with Amalek, the cruel enemy of his people, Deuteronomy 25:17-19, the perpetual judgment, the war until Amalek's destruction, 1 Samuel 15); the LORD's oath (the solemn, irrevocable decree of war against Amalek) — the divine enmity sworn (the certainty of the judgment); 'war with Amalek from generation to generation' (the perpetual war, until Amalek's utter destruction, fulfilled through the generations, Saul, David, Hezekiah's days, 1 Chronicles 4:43, the judgment on the enemy of God's people, the type of God's perpetual war against the enemies of his church, the powers of darkness, until their final overthrow, Revelation 19:11-21, the certainty of the doom) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Calvin, Gill.

themes: For he said, Because the LORD hath sworn that the LORD will have war with Amalek from generation to generation
the LORD's perpetual war with Amalek
God himself at war with the cruel enemy of his people (Deuteronomy 25:17-19; 1 Samuel 15)
a type of God's war against the enemies of his church until their final overthrow (Revelation 19:11-21)
